questions.text: I find that once an emotion takes hold, it commands my full attention.
I believe that the felt weight of another person's hardship is a form of moral evidence that deserves serious consideration.
I can be in the middle of a crisis and still feel internally composed.
I tend to view my savings as a tool for my own future, not a resource for others.
I tend to think of my time as my most valuable personal asset.
I tend to absorb emotional information without it disrupting my internal stability.
I do not naturally sense when someone is testing boundaries.
I am meticulous about keeping my appointments and rarely cancel or reschedule.
I often start projects without a clear plan, trusting that the path will reveal itself.
I feel that attending to my own desires while others have unmet needs is hard to justify.
I notice micro-expressions that flash across someone's face during conversation.
I rarely register the emotional atmosphere of a gathering.
I feel that if I don't take the reins, things won't move forward.
I believe that remaining analytically engaged while others are emotionally overwhelmed is a contribution that often goes unrecognized.
I trust the group's consensus more than my own individual judgment.
Five-year plans seem pointless to me because so much changes that they quickly become irrelevant.
I find it natural to sit back and let the group work through its process.
I find that I am drawn to roles and situations where my capacity for clear thinking during emotional intensity is needed.
